meta.certificationName: Microsoft Certified: Azure MLOps Engineer Associate (AI-300)
meta.title: MS-AI300-4: Reusable Assets, Deployment Controls, and Monitoring

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to start an MLflow tracking run and log a hyperparameter called `learning_rate` with value 0.01. Which code is correct?

options.explanation: Mlflow.log_param() is the correct function for logging a single key-value parameter within an active run context opened by mlflow.start_run().
Mlflow.log_metric() is for numeric evaluation metrics (which can be tracked over time with steps), not hyperparameters.
Mlflow.set_param() is not a valid MLflow function.
Mlflow.track() is not a valid MLflow function.

questions.reference: [Log metrics, parameters, and files with MLflow - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-log-view-metrics)

questions.stem:
A data scientist runs the following code:

```python
import mlflow
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

X, y = load_iris(return_X_y=True)
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, random_state=42)

mlflow.sklearn.autolog()

with mlflow.start_run():
    clf = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, max_depth=5)
    clf.fit(X_train, y_train)
```

Which items are automatically logged by MLflow autologging for sklearn?

options.text: Model parameters (n_estimators, max_depth), training metrics, and the fitted model as an artifact
options.explanation: Mlflow.sklearn.autolog() automatically captures estimator parameters (such as n_estimators and max_depth), training metrics (such as training accuracy), and saves the fitted model as a logged artifact.

options.text: Model parameters only; metrics and artifacts must be logged manually
options.explanation: Autolog captures more than just parameters — it also logs training metrics and the trained model artifact.

options.text: The fitted model artifact only; parameters and metrics must be logged manually
options.explanation: Autolog captures parameters and metrics, not just the model artifact.

options.text: Model parameters, training metrics, the fitted model, and the raw training dataset
options.explanation: Autolog does NOT automatically log the raw training dataset; that must be done manually with mlflow.log_artifact() or the MLflow datasets API.

questions.reference: [Configure MLflow for Azure Machine Learning - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-use-mlflow-configure-tracking)

questions.stem: A data scientist is using GridSearchCV on a pipeline with StandardScaler and LogisticRegression. The data has 10,000 rows and cv=5. Why is it important to include StandardScaler inside the pipeline rather than fitting it on the full dataset before GridSearchCV?

options.text: StandardScaler cannot be used with GridSearchCV
options.explanation: If StandardScaler is fit on the entire dataset before cross-validation, the scaler learns mean and variance from validation fold data that the model should not have seen during training. This constitutes data leakage, leading to overly optimistic performance estimates. By placing StandardScaler inside the pipeline, GridSearchCV ensures the scaler is fit only on training folds during each CV iteration.

options.text: Including StandardScaler in the pipeline makes cross-validation run faster
options.explanation: StandardScaler works perfectly within sklearn pipelines and GridSearchCV — it is one of the primary use cases.

options.text: StandardScaler must be fit separately before being passed to a pipeline
options.explanation: The pipeline does not speed up cross-validation; it actually adds overhead due to refitting the scaler on each fold.

options.text: Fitting StandardScaler before cross-validation causes data leakage because the scaler learns statistics from validation fold data
options.explanation: StandardScaler is designed to be used inside pipelines and does not need to be pre-fit before being added.

questions.reference: [Train scikit-learn machine learning models (v2) - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-train-scikit-learn)

questions.stem: A team needs to use Azure Machine Learning automated ML for a regression task. Select TWO limitations that apply to Azure Machine Learning automated ML. (Select TWO)

options.explanation: Azure ML automated ML does support time-series forecasting as a task type.

options.text: AutoML does not support custom loss functions
options.explanation: Azure ML automated ML uses a fixed set of algorithms and evaluation metrics; users cannot inject custom loss functions into AutoML trials.

options.text: AutoML only supports structured (tabular) data, not unstructured data like raw text or images natively
options.explanation: AutoML is designed for structured tabular data and does not natively handle unstructured inputs such as raw image pixels or free-form text without prior feature extraction.

options.text: AutoML cannot log runs to MLflow
options.explanation: AutoML automatically logs all trial runs to MLflow and can register the best model in the model registry.

questions.reference: [What is automated ML? AutoML - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/concept-automated-ml)

questions.stem: Why do Azure ML environments matter in CI/CD?

options.text: They package consistent runtime dependencies for repeatable jobs and deployments
options.explanation: Environments define the runtime stack used by jobs and deployments, which is central to repeatability.

options.text: They replace every need for Docker or conda metadata
options.explanation: They often rely on Docker and dependency specs rather than replacing them conceptually.

options.text: They are only visual labels in Studio
options.explanation: They have operational effect beyond labeling.

options.text: They can be ignored once a model is registered
options.explanation: Registered models still need a serving environment.

questions.reference: [Azure ML compute targets](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/concept-compute-target)

questions.stem: Which of the following machine learning libraries comes pre-installed in the Azure Machine Learning curated environment?

options.explanation: The Azure Machine Learning curated environments pre-install a curated set of ML libraries including scikit-learn, MLflow, XGBoost, LightGBM, TensorFlow, and PyTorch, enabling data scientists to start training without managing dependencies.
Apache Kafka is a streaming platform, not an ML library, and is not bundled in Azure ML curated environments.
Apache Flink is a separate stream-processing engine not included in Azure ML curated environments.
Apache Hudi is a data lake storage format, not an ML library, and is not part of Azure ML curated environments.

questions.reference: [Curated environments - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/resource-curated-environments)

questions.stem: What is a practical role for GitHub Actions in Azure ML MLOps?

options.text: Automating training, validation, or deployment workflows from repository events
options.explanation: GitHub Actions can orchestrate CI/CD pipelines that trigger Azure ML jobs and deployments.

options.text: Replacing the Azure ML control plane
options.explanation: Azure ML remains the ML platform control plane.
Predictions are served by endpoints.
Actions do not replace a registry.

questions.reference: [Use GitHub Actions with Azure ML](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-github-actions-machine-learning?view=azureml-api-2)

questions.stem: What is the Responsible AI dashboard primarily used for?

options.text: Inspecting fairness, explanations, error patterns, and related model behaviors
options.explanation: The Responsible AI dashboard brings together explanations, fairness, error analysis, and similar assessments.
Cluster creation is unrelated.
It does not train foundation models.
It complements rather than replaces experiment tracking.

questions.reference: [Responsible AI dashboard](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/concept-responsible-ai-dashboard?view=azureml-api-2)

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to track the hyperparameters and evaluation metrics for a model training run using MLflow. Which two function calls should they use?

options.text: mlflow.record_param() for hyperparameters and mlflow.record_metric() for metrics
options.explanation: MLflow provides distinct functions: mlflow.log_param() is used for logging scalar hyperparameters (e.g., learning rate, max depth) and mlflow.log_metric() is used for logging numeric evaluation metrics (e.g., accuracy, RMSE) that can be tracked over time with optional steps.

options.text: mlflow.log_param() for both hyperparameters and metrics
options.explanation: Mlflow.record_param() and mlflow.record_metric() are not valid MLflow functions.

options.text: mlflow.set_tag() for hyperparameters and mlflow.log_metric() for metrics
options.explanation: Mlflow.log_param() is only for parameters, not metrics — log_metric() must be used for numeric evaluation results.

options.text: mlflow.log_param() for hyperparameters and mlflow.log_metric() for metrics
options.explanation: Mlflow.set_tag() stores string key-value metadata tags, not numeric hyperparameter values.

questions.reference: [Log metrics, parameters, and files with MLflow - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-log-view-metrics)

questions.stem: A practitioner uses mlflow.models.infer_signature() to record a model signature. Select TWO reasons why recording a model signature is important for production deployments. (Select TWO)

options.text: It enforces input/output schema validation at serving time, preventing silent type mismatches
options.explanation: A model signature defines the expected input columns and types; MLflow serving enforces this schema and rejects malformed requests at inference time, preventing silent data type mismatches.

options.text: It automatically retrains the model when input distributions shift
options.explanation: Signatures do not trigger retraining — that is the responsibility of monitoring and alerting pipelines.

options.text: It compresses the model artifact to reduce storage costs
options.explanation: Azure ML online endpoints use the signature to auto-generate API documentation showing expected input and output shapes in the deployment UI and REST schema.

options.text: It enables Azure Machine Learning online endpoints to generate accurate API documentation for the endpoint
options.explanation: Signatures are metadata stored alongside the model and have no effect on model file size or compression.

questions.reference: [Manage models registry with MLflow - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-manage-models-mlflow)

questions.stem: A practitioner sets up autologging with `mlflow.autolog()` before training a scikit-learn Pipeline that includes a StandardScaler and a LogisticRegression. Which option is TRUE about what gets logged?

options.text: mlflow.autolog() does not work with sklearn Pipelines; each stage must be logged manually
options.explanation: Sklearn autologging is pipeline-aware: it logs parameters from all stages in the pipeline (using the stage_name__param_name convention), not just the final estimator. Training metrics are also captured automatically.

options.text: Only the final estimator's parameters (LogisticRegression) are logged; preprocessor parameters are ignored
options.explanation: Sklearn autologging extracts and logs parameters from all named steps in the pipeline, including preprocessors like StandardScaler.

options.text: Only metrics are logged; all parameters must be logged with mlflow.log_param()
options.explanation: Sklearn autologging fully supports Pipeline objects; it is one of the primary use cases for the feature.

options.text: All pipeline stage parameters (including StandardScaler's with_mean, LogisticRegression's C and max_iter) and training metrics are logged automatically
options.explanation: Autologging captures both parameters and metrics automatically — no manual log_param() calls are needed for pipeline parameters.

questions.reference: [Configure MLflow for Azure Machine Learning - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-use-mlflow-configure-tracking)

questions.stem: You need to choose the most appropriate action. which MLflow function is used to log a scalar metric value (such as validation RMSE) during a training run?

options.explanation: Log_param() logs a hyperparameter string or number but is meant for configuration values, not evaluation metrics tracked over time.
Log_artifact() logs a file or directory as an artifact, not a scalar numeric value.
Mlflow.log_metric() is specifically for logging numeric scalar values (e.g., accuracy, RMSE) that can be plotted over steps in the Azure ML experiment UI.
Set_tag() stores free-text metadata tags on a run, not numeric metrics.

questions.reference: [Log metrics, parameters, and files with MLflow - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-log-view-metrics)

questions.stem: An ML engineer is running a hyperparameter sweep where each combination spawns its own MLflow run, but all should be grouped under a single parent run for easy comparison. What is the correct pattern?

options.text: Use with mlflow.start_run() as parent, then call mlflow.start_run(nested=True) for each child trial
options.explanation: Nested=False would not create a child relationship — the runs would be siblings, not children of the parent.

options.text: Set the same run_name for all runs using mlflow.set_tag('run_name', parent_id)
options.explanation: The standard MLflow pattern for hyperparameter sweeps is to open a parent run with mlflow.start_run() and then open each child trial with mlflow.start_run(nested=True) inside a loop. This creates the parent-child run hierarchy visible in the Azure ML experiment UI.

options.text: Log all child metrics to the parent run using mlflow.log_metrics() with a prefix
options.explanation: Run_name is just metadata and does not establish a parent-child hierarchy in the MLflow run tree.

options.text: Call mlflow.start_run() once for all child runs and pass nested=False to each
options.explanation: Logging all metrics to the parent run loses the per-trial granularity that makes hyperparameter comparison meaningful.

questions.reference: [Configure MLflow for Azure Machine Learning - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-use-mlflow-configure-tracking)

questions.stem: A bank must host inference inside its own AKS environment because of enterprise network controls. Which of the following endpoint style is the best fit?

options.text: A Kubernetes online endpoint on AKS
options.explanation: Kubernetes online endpoints support AKS-based serving for organizations that need cluster-level control.

options.text: A managed online endpoint with no network planning
options.explanation: Managed endpoints reduce ops but may not satisfy that AKS requirement.
Batch endpoints are asynchronous rather than interactive.
A registry stores assets but does not serve them.

questions.reference: [Azure ML online endpoints](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/concept-endpoints-online?view=azureml-api-2)

questions.stem: A team wants the same pipeline definition to run against different input paths and hyperparameters without editing code. Which design supports that best?

options.text: Hard-code every path and setting in the script
options.explanation: Pipeline parameters let the same definition run flexibly across environments and inputs.

options.text: Create a new workspace for each dataset
options.explanation: Hard-coding hurts reuse.
Separate workspaces are unnecessary for parameter variation.

options.text: Declare pipeline inputs and runtime parameters
options.explanation: Manual clicks are not the most reproducible design.

questions.reference: [Azure ML pipelines and components](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-create-component-pipelines-cli)

questions.stem: A data scientist is building a churn prediction model and wants to encode a high-cardinality categorical feature (e.g., zip code with 1,000 unique values). Which encoding strategy is most appropriate?

options.text: Target encoding or hashing, to avoid creating 1,000 binary columns
options.explanation: Target encoding summarizes a categorical feature with target-related statistics (e.g., mean of the target per category), and hashing encodes values into a fixed-size vector, both of which handle high-cardinality features without dimensional explosion.

options.text: OneHotEncoder, to create one binary column per unique value
options.explanation: OneHotEncoding 1,000 unique zip codes would create 1,000 binary columns, causing the curse of dimensionality and memory issues.

options.text: OrdinalEncoder assigning integers 0–999 and treating them as numeric
options.explanation: Assigning arbitrary ordinal integers to nominal categories like zip codes implies a false ordering that distance-based models and linear models may misinterpret.

options.text: No encoding is needed; tree models handle categorical strings natively in sklearn
options.explanation: Scikit-learn estimators do not natively handle string categoricals; encoding is required before fitting.

questions.reference: [Train scikit-learn machine learning models (v2) - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-train-scikit-learn)

questions.stem: An ML team wants to use the Azure ML model registry to manage model lifecycle. Select TWO valid operations the model registry supports. (Select TWO)

options.text: Transitioning a model version from Staging to Production
options.explanation: One of the core model registry features is the ability to transition model versions through lifecycle stages (None → Staging → Production → Archived) to manage deployment promotion.

options.text: Comparing metrics across different versions of a registered model
options.explanation: The MLflow UI and API allow users to view and compare metrics, parameters, and artifacts logged across different versions of a registered model.

options.text: Automatically retraining a model when new data arrives
options.explanation: The model registry does not trigger or manage automated retraining; that requires external orchestration (e.g., Azure ML pipelines, Azure DevOps).

options.text: Serving the model as a REST API with zero configuration
options.explanation: While Azure ML online endpoints can deploy registered models, the model registry itself does not automatically expose a REST API with zero configuration.

questions.reference: [MLOps machine learning model management - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/concept-model-management-and-deployment)

questions.stem: A data scientist needs to scale numeric features to have zero mean and unit variance before training a Support Vector Machine. Which sklearn transformer should they use?

options.explanation: MinMaxScaler scales features to a fixed range (default [0, 1]) but does not produce zero mean and unit variance.

StandardScaler standardizes features by subtracting the mean and dividing by the standard deviation, resulting in features with zero mean and unit variance — the canonical preprocessing for SVMs and other distance-based algorithms.
LabelEncoder converts class labels to integer indices; it is used for target encoding, not feature scaling.
Normalizer scales each sample (row) to unit norm, not each feature (column) to zero mean and unit variance.

questions.reference: [Train scikit-learn machine learning models (v2) - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-train-scikit-learn)

questions.stem: A practitioner needs to create a train/test split while ensuring that the proportion of each class label is preserved in both sets. Which sklearn function and parameter achieves this?

options.explanation: The stratify parameter in train_test_split accepts the target array y and ensures that each split preserves the same class distribution as the original dataset, which is critical for imbalanced problems.
Shuffle=False disables shuffling before splitting, which is useful for time series data but does not control class proportions.
Random_state only controls reproducibility of the random split, not class distribution across splits.
Balanced=True is not a valid parameter for train_test_split — it does not exist in sklearn.

questions.reference: [Train scikit-learn machine learning models (v2) - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-train-scikit-learn)

questions.stem: An ML engineer wants to register an existing MLflow run's model in the Azure ML model registry. The run ID is `abc123` and the model was logged to the path `model`. Which code achieves this?

options.explanation: Mlflow.register_model() takes a model URI in the form 'runs:/<run_id>/<artifact_path>' and a registered model name, creating a new version in the model registry linked to that run.
Mlflow.log_model() is used during training to log a model object; it does not register an already-logged model from a past run.
Mlflow.Model.register() is not a valid MLflow function.
Mlflow.tracking.register() is not a valid MLflow function.

questions.reference: [Manage models registry with MLflow - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-manage-models-mlflow)

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to solve the following problem. a data scientist wants to record a SHAP summary plot as part of an MLflow run. After generating the matplotlib figure object `fig`, which call correctly logs it to the active run?

options.explanation: Log_metric() only accepts numeric scalars, not figure objects.
Log_artifact() requires a file path string, not a figure object directly.
Mlflow.log_figure() accepts a matplotlib or plotly figure object and a destination filename, saving it as an artifact in the run without requiring the caller to save the file to disk first.
Log_param() stores string or numeric hyperparameters, not figures.

questions.reference: [Log metrics, parameters, and files with MLflow - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-log-view-metrics)

questions.stem: A data scientist enables autologging with mlflow.autolog() before training a scikit-learn model. Select TWO items that are NOT automatically logged by sklearn autologging. (Select TWO)

options.explanation: Model hyperparameters are automatically captured by sklearn autologging.

options.text: Custom business metrics computed after prediction (e.g., revenue impact)
options.explanation: Custom business metrics calculated outside the model training loop (such as revenue impact from predictions) are not captured by autologging; they require explicit mlflow.log_metric() calls.

options.text: Training dataset schema and raw data
options.explanation: Sklearn autologging does not log the raw training dataset or its schema; only model parameters and built-in training metrics are captured automatically.

options.text: Test set evaluation metrics computed via model.score()
options.explanation: Test set metrics are logged when the user calls model.score() or cross_val_score() inside an active autolog context.

questions.reference: [Configure MLflow for Azure Machine Learning - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-use-mlflow-configure-tracking)

questions.stem: An analytics team is comparing two models logged in MLflow. Model A has val_auc=0.87 logged in run abc123, and Model B has val_auc=0.91 in run def456. Using the MlflowClient, which method would return a list of runs for programmatic comparison?

options.explanation: Get_run() retrieves a single run by ID — not useful for multi-run comparison.
MlflowClient().search_runs() returns a list of run objects filtered by metric thresholds, params, or tags, enabling programmatic comparison across multiple runs in an experiment.
List_artifacts() returns file artifacts for a single run, not metric comparisons across runs.
Get_experiment_by_name() returns experiment metadata but not the runs within it.

questions.reference: [Query & compare experiments and runs with MLflow - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-mlflow)

questions.stem:
An ML engineer registers a newly trained model in the Azure Machine Learning model registry with the following code:

```python
mlflow.sklearn.log_model(
    sk_model=model,
    artifact_path='model',
    registered_model_name='churn_predictor'
)
```

What stage will this model version be in immediately after registration?

options.explanation: When a model is first registered in the Azure ML model registry (whether via log_model with registered_model_name or mlflow.register_model()), its initial stage is 'None'. The engineer must explicitly transition it to Staging or Production using MlflowClient().transition_model_version_stage().
Staging must be explicitly set after registration — it is not assigned automatically.
Production must be explicitly set and is not assigned automatically on registration.
Development is not a valid stage in the Azure ML model registry.

questions.reference: [MLOps machine learning model management - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/concept-model-management-and-deployment)

questions.stem: A data scientist sets up autologging with `mlflow.autolog()` before training a scikit-learn Pipeline that includes a StandardScaler and a LogisticRegression. Which of the following is TRUE about what gets logged?

options.text: All pipeline stage parameters (including StandardScaler's with_mean, LogisticRegression's C and max_iter) and training metrics are logged automatically
options.explanation: Sklearn autologging is pipeline-aware: it logs parameters from all stages in the pipeline (using the stage_name__param_name convention), not just the final estimator. Training metrics are also captured automatically.

options.text: Only the final estimator's parameters (LogisticRegression) are logged; preprocessor parameters are ignored
options.explanation: Sklearn autologging extracts and logs parameters from all named steps in the pipeline, including preprocessors like StandardScaler.

options.text: mlflow.autolog() does not work with sklearn Pipelines; each stage must be logged manually
options.explanation: Sklearn autologging fully supports Pipeline objects; it is one of the primary use cases for the feature.

options.text: Only metrics are logged; all parameters must be logged with mlflow.log_param()
options.explanation: Autologging captures both parameters and metrics automatically — no manual log_param() calls are needed for pipeline parameters.

questions.reference: [Configure MLflow for Azure Machine Learning - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-use-mlflow-configure-tracking)

questions.stem: A data scientist logs a file called `confusion_matrix.png` during a training run. Which MLflow call is correct?

options.explanation: Mlflow.log_artifact() is used to save local files (images, plots, CSVs, etc.) to the run's artifact store, making them available for review in the Azure ML experiment UI.
Mlflow.log_metric() is for numeric scalar values, not file paths.
Mlflow.log_param() stores string key-value pairs describing hyperparameters, not files.
Mlflow.log_model() is for serializing and logging a trained ML model object, not an image file.

questions.reference: [Log metrics, parameters, and files with MLflow - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-log-view-metrics)

questions.stem: You need to choose the most appropriate action. a data scientist is comparing Azure Machine Learning automated ML to manually building a model. Select TWO TRUE statements about Azure Machine Learning automated ML. (Select TWO)

options.text: AutoML supports custom neural network architectures defined in PyTorch
options.explanation: Azure ML automated ML systematically explores a configurable set of algorithms (e.g., LightGBM, scikit-learn models, XGBoost) and hyperparameter combinations, logging each trial as a separate MLflow run for full observability.

options.text: AutoML generates a source notebook for the best run that can be cloned and customized
options.explanation: At the end of an AutoML experiment, Azure ML generates an editable Python notebook containing the full code for the best run, which data scientists can clone, inspect, and customize.

options.text: AutoML automatically handles feature engineering for all data types including unstructured text and images
options.explanation: AutoML does not support custom neural network architectures; it uses a fixed set of supported classical ML and ensemble algorithms.

options.text: AutoML explores multiple algorithms and hyperparameter combinations, logging each as a separate MLflow run
options.explanation: AutoML is designed for structured tabular data and does not perform feature engineering on unstructured data such as raw images or free-form text.

questions.reference: [What is automated ML? AutoML - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/concept-automated-ml)

questions.stem: Which Azure Machine Learning compute type is most appropriate for an individual engineer interactively developing notebooks?

options.explanation: Compute instances are intended for interactive development such as notebooks and ad hoc authoring.
Online endpoints serve inference traffic, not notebook authoring.
Batch endpoints run asynchronous scoring jobs.
A registry stores shareable assets rather than interactive compute.

questions.reference: [Azure ML compute targets](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/concept-compute-target)

questions.stem: An analytics team migrates from the classic MLflow model registry (Staging/Production stages) to the newer Azure ML registry with model aliases. What is the correct way to load the model version tagged as the champion model using aliases?

options.explanation: 'Production' is a stage from the classic registry, not an alias — in the newer alias-based approach, you use @ syntax instead.
The newer model alias feature uses the @ syntax to reference a named alias: models:/ModelName@alias. This is the correct format for loading a model version by alias in the Azure ML model registry.
Get_model_version_by_alias() returns version metadata but does not load the model for inference — a separate load_model() call is still needed.
Models:/MyModel/champion uses the slash format which refers to stages, not aliases. Aliases require the @ syntax.

questions.reference: [Manage models registry with MLflow - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-manage-models-mlflow)

questions.stem: A generative workflow built in Azure wants prompt orchestration, testing, and execution of flow graphs. Which of the following tool family is most aligned?

options.explanation: Prompt flow is designed for authoring and evaluating prompt-based AI flows.
Batch endpoints are for asynchronous scoring jobs.
Registry tags are metadata only.
Datastore credentials are storage configuration.

questions.reference: [Prompt flow in Azure AI Foundry](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-foundry/how-to/flow-develop)

options.text: mlflow.log_param() for hyperparameters and mlflow.log_metric() for metrics
options.explanation: MLflow provides distinct functions: mlflow.log_param() is used for logging scalar hyperparameters (e.g., learning rate, max depth) and mlflow.log_metric() is used for logging numeric evaluation metrics (e.g., accuracy, RMSE) that can be tracked over time with optional steps.

options.text: mlflow.record_param() for hyperparameters and mlflow.record_metric() for metrics
options.explanation: Mlflow.record_param() and mlflow.record_metric() are not valid MLflow functions.

options.text: mlflow.set_tag() for hyperparameters and mlflow.log_metric() for metrics
options.explanation: Mlflow.log_param() is only for parameters, not metrics — log_metric() must be used for numeric evaluation results.

options.text: mlflow.log_param() for both hyperparameters and metrics
options.explanation: Mlflow.set_tag() stores string key-value metadata tags, not numeric hyperparameter values.

questions.reference: [Log metrics, parameters, and files with MLflow - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-log-view-metrics)

questions.stem: Which of the following Azure Machine Learning compute type is most appropriate for an individual engineer interactively developing notebooks?

options.explanation: Compute instances are intended for interactive development such as notebooks and ad hoc authoring.
Online endpoints serve inference traffic, not notebook authoring.
Batch endpoints run asynchronous scoring jobs.
A registry stores shareable assets rather than interactive compute.

questions.reference: [Azure ML compute targets](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/concept-compute-target)

questions.stem: Which data asset type is intended for tabular or structured data with schema semantics in Azure ML?

options.explanation: MLTable is designed for structured datasets with transformations and schema information.
URI file is a generic file reference rather than a tabular abstraction.
An environment packages software dependencies.
An online endpoint is for inference serving.

questions.reference: [Azure ML data concepts](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/concept-data)

questions.stem: An ML engineer has trained a model and wants to register it in the Azure Machine Learning model registry so it can be promoted through stages. Which function registers a logged model run artifact to the registry?

options.explanation: Log_model() saves the model artifact to the current run but does not register it in the model registry.
Mlflow.register_model() takes a run artifact URI (runs:/<run_id>/<artifact_path>) and a registry model name, creating a new version in the model registry linked to that run.
Create_registered_model() creates an empty registered model entry in the registry without associating any run artifact or version.
Save_model() saves the model to a local path and has no model registry interaction.

questions.reference: [Manage models registry with MLflow - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-manage-models-mlflow)

questions.stem: Which HyperOpt search space expression should a data scientist use to define a continuous uniform distribution between 0.001 and 0.1 for a learning rate hyperparameter?

options.explanation: Hp.choice() picks from a discrete list — correct if you only want those three values, but not a continuous distribution.
Hp.uniform('lr', low, high) samples floating-point values uniformly between low and high, which is the standard choice for continuous hyperparameters like learning rate.
Hp.randint() samples integers from 0 to n, not floats in a continuous range.
Hp.quniform() samples at discrete intervals (quantized uniform), not a truly continuous distribution.

questions.reference: [Hyperparameter tuning a model (v2) - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-tune-hyperparameters)

questions.stem: A training workload must scale out for repeated jobs and stop when idle to control cost. Which of the following compute target is the best default choice?

options.explanation: Compute clusters are the standard elastic target for scheduled and repeated training jobs.

options.text: A local laptop attached as a target
options.explanation: Instances are better for interactive work than pooled training jobs.
A local machine is not the best managed default for scalable production jobs.
Registries are not execution targets.

questions.reference: [Azure ML compute targets](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/concept-compute-target)

questions.stem: A practitioner enables MLflow autologging before training a scikit-learn RandomForestClassifier. Which option artifacts is NOT automatically logged by mlflow.sklearn.autolog()?

options.explanation: Model parameters are automatically captured by mlflow.sklearn.autolog().

options.text: The trained model as an MLflow artifact
options.explanation: Training metrics are automatically captured by mlflow.sklearn.autolog().

options.text: Custom business KPIs defined outside the training function
options.explanation: The trained model artifact is automatically saved by mlflow.sklearn.autolog().

Autologging only captures what the sklearn framework itself produces — parameters, standard metrics, and the trained model. Custom business KPIs computed outside the sklearn training loop (e.g., revenue impact, SLA compliance) must be logged manually with mlflow.log_metric().

questions.reference: [Configure MLflow for Azure Machine Learning - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-use-mlflow-configure-tracking)

questions.stem: An ML engineer wants to load a registered model and run predictions using a generic Python interface that works regardless of the original training framework. Which loading function should they use?

options.explanation: Mlflow.sklearn.load_model() is a framework-specific loader that only works for sklearn models and returns a native sklearn object.
Mlflow.tensorflow.load_model() is a framework-specific loader that only works for TensorFlow models.

Mlflow.pyfunc.load_model() loads any MLflow model using the generic Python function flavor, exposing a .predict() method regardless of the original framework (sklearn, XGBoost, TensorFlow, etc.). This makes downstream code framework-agnostic.
Mlflow.load_model() is not a valid top-level MLflow function — pyfunc.load_model() is the correct generic interface.

questions.reference: [Guidelines for Deploying MLflow Models - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-deploy-mlflow-models)

questions.stem: A pipeline must fan out scoring across partitions and then merge outputs. Which design is most aligned with Azure ML capabilities?

options.text: Put every partition in a single monolithic script step by hand
options.explanation: Azure ML pipelines support reusable components and patterns for parallelizable job design.

options.text: Avoid versioning so each run can change freely
options.explanation: A monolithic hand-built step is harder to scale and govern.

options.text: Use only a model registry with no pipeline job
options.explanation: Avoiding versioning hurts MLOps discipline.

options.text: Use reusable components and parallel-capable pipeline steps
options.explanation: A registry alone does not execute pipelines.

questions.reference: [Azure ML pipelines and components](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-create-component-pipelines-cli)

questions.stem: A practitioner is building a time-series model to predict daily sales. They need to create train and test sets. Why should they NOT use random shuffling when splitting this dataset?

options.text: Random shuffling reduces the size of the training set
options.explanation: Time-series data has a temporal dependency structure: future values are correlated with past values. Random shuffling mixes future observations into the training set, allowing the model to learn from data it would not have access to in production. The correct approach is a chronological split.

options.text: Random shuffling breaks the temporal order, causing future data to leak into the training set, which inflates performance estimates
options.explanation: Class imbalance is a separate issue unrelated to the temporal ordering problem in time-series splitting.

options.text: sklearn's train_test_split does not support time-series data
options.explanation: Sklearn's train_test_split works with any array; however, it must be called with shuffle=False for time-series data to maintain chronological order.

options.text: Random shuffling causes class imbalance in time-series data
options.explanation: The size of the train/test split is controlled by the test_size parameter, not by whether shuffling is used.

questions.reference: [Set up AutoML with Python (v2) - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-configure-auto-train)
